beats.text: Here's a three-by-three grid floating in space — call it matrix A.

Its columns are arrows: one pointing to two, one, zero and another to one, zero, one. Every linear combination of those arrows traces out a plane through the origin. That plane is the column space — every target A can actually reach.
Now shrink one column until it's redundant. The plane collapses to a line through the origin. Still a subspace — you can't escape it by adding or scaling.
Flip the question: what inputs x send A times x straight to zero? Those vectors form the null space — an arrow landing exactly at the origin.
Column space shows what's reachable. Null space reveals what disappears. Together, they expose every power and every limit hiding inside A.
